Name: kv_cache_layout_optimization
Title: Optimizing KV-Cache Layout for Efficient Large Language Model Inference
Short Hypothesis: Reorganizing the KV-cache layout to minimize memory bandwidth usage and improve tensor core occupancy will significantly reduce wall-clock latency for large language model inference on commodity GPUs.
Related Work: Recent work on LLM optimization has focused on quantization (AMP-ViT, SNIP) and mixed-precision training (EMQ), but these approaches do not address the specific hardware bottlenecks caused by inefficient KV-cache layouts. While HiLoRA introduces adaptive hierarchical routing, it does not optimize memory access patterns. This proposal uniquely targets the KV-cache layout, leveraging empirical profiling to achieve measurable speedups.
Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) rely on key-value (KV) caches to store intermediate states during inference, but the default layout of these caches often leads to inefficient memory access patterns, resulting in high bandwidth usage and low tensor core occupancy. This paper proposes a novel approach to reorganize the KV-cache layout based on empirical profiling of memory access patterns and hardware utilization metrics. By minimizing memory bandwidth usage and maximizing tensor core occupancy, our method aims to significantly reduce wall-clock latency for LLM inference on commodity GPUs. We validate our approach through extensive profiling and A/B latency tests, demonstrating measurable speedups while maintaining model accuracy.
Experiments: Profile the default KV-cache layout using nvprof/Nsight to measure memory bandwidth usage, tensor core occupancy, and warp stall reasons.
Design and implement a reorganizeKVcache function that optimizes the layout based on profiling data, focusing on coalesced loads and reduced HBM round-trips.
Conduct A/B latency tests comparing the default layout with the optimized layout, measuring p50/p99 latency, throughput under batching, and energy per token.
Validate the optimized layout across multiple GPUs/driver versions, reporting variance across runs as measured jitter.
Compare the optimized layout with existing quantization and mixed-precision methods (AMP-ViT, SNIP, EMQ) to demonstrate complementary benefits.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The optimized layout may introduce additional overhead for small batch sizes or short sequences, limiting its effectiveness in certain scenarios.
The approach may require re-engineering the KV-cache management logic, potentially complicating integration with existing frameworks.
The benefits of the optimized layout may vary across different GPU architectures, requiring architecture-specific tuning.
Empirical profiling may not capture all edge cases, leading to suboptimal layouts for certain workloads.
